Now I have the standardPlace. Let me load the remaining survey tools and run the full parallel batch.Now I'll fire all survey calls in parallel.Let me now read the specific NYC Genealogy wiki page returned by the search.I now have all tool results needed to compile the guide. Here it is:

---

# Locality Guide: New York City, New York (1700–1799)

## Jurisdiction overview

- **Place:** New York City (standardPlace: *New York, New York, United States*), occupying Manhattan Island and constituting New York County throughout the colonial period
- **Date range in FamilySearch:** 1624 onward
- **Administrative center:** City of New York; also the seat of New York Province under British rule
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Province of New York (British Crown colony, 1664–1783); State of New York (from 1788)
- **Population during period:** No historical population data was returned by the FamilySearch population tool for this period. Consult the New-York Historical Society or Wikipedia's account of colonial NYC demographics directly.
- **Economy:** Major commercial seaport and trade hub; merchant commerce, shipping, artisanal crafts, and finance dominated
- **Ethnic composition:** Dutch (a significant residual community from the New Amsterdam era), English, Scots-Irish, French Huguenot, Jewish (Sephardic), and enslaved African residents. Per the Wikipedia entry, the city transitioned from a Dutch colonial population to a predominantly English one after 1664.
- **Religious denominations:** Dutch Reformed (oldest continuous records), Anglican/Church of England (Trinity Church was the dominant Anglican congregation), Presbyterian, French Reformed (Huguenot), Jewish (Shearith Israel congregation, the oldest in North America), and Lutheran, per the FamilySearch Wiki "New York City Genealogy" article and the Wikipedia entry
- **Key historical events:** British takeover from the Dutch (1664); French and Indian War (1754–1763); American Revolution (1775–1783) — British occupation of NYC lasted 1776–1783, disrupting record-keeping; NYC briefly served as the first U.S. capital (1785–1790)

---

## Boundary changes

- New York City occupied Manhattan Island as New York County throughout the entire 1700s. No significant boundary changes affect record custody during this period.
- The political transition from British Province of New York to the State of New York (1788) transferred administrative authority but did not alter the county structure or relocate existing records.
- Records created before 1783 fall under **colonial/provincial authority** (New York Province); records from 1783 onward fall under state authority. The Prerogative Court of New York handled provincial probate throughout the colonial period for the entire province.

---

## Available record types

### Vital records (civil registration)
- **Start date:** ⚠️ Civil registration of births, marriages, and deaths did **not** exist in New York City during the 1700s. County-level marriage registration began 1847–1849 (a collection covering this is on FamilySearch, but its date range is 1847–1936 — entirely outside the colonial period). Statewide civil birth and death registration was not mandated until well after this period.
- **Pre-registration alternatives:** Church records are the **only** systematic source for baptisms, marriages, and burials in this period. See Church Records section below.

### Church records
This is the **most important record category** for 1700s NYC families.

| Denomination | Notes | Digitization level |
|---|---|---|
| **Dutch Reformed** | Oldest continuous series, beginning 1639. FamilySearch holds **1 digitized volume** (imageGroupNumber 007733180, 211 images, covering 1700–1776). ⚠️ **Browse-only — 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable. Records are in Dutch and require Dutch-language reading ability.** | Browse-only images (non-English) |
| **Anglican / Church of England** | Trinity Church registers. Per the FamilySearch Wiki "New York City Genealogy" article, these records are held by Trinity Church Wall Street and have been partially published. | Physical / partially published |
| **Presbyterian** | Per the FamilySearch Wiki article, Presbyterian records exist for the colonial period | Physical / check denominational archives |
| **French Reformed (Huguenot)** | The French Protestant community maintained separate registers | Physical / check Huguenot Society |
| **Jewish (Shearith Israel)** | Congregation Shearith Israel (1654–) is the oldest Jewish congregation in North America; records exist | Physical / contact congregation |

The broader **FamilySearch collection "New York, Church Records, 1640–1940"** (collection ID 1469062) covers the target period and contains approximately 2.3 million records with roughly 1.1 million associated images at the New York state level. This is indexed and is the most accessible online starting point for church record searching.

- **Access:** FamilySearch.org — [collection 1469062](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1469062) (indexed + images, free)
- **Physical repositories:** Individual denominational archives; the New-York Historical Society and NY Genealogical & Biographical Society hold significant holdings per the FamilySearch Wiki "New York City Genealogy" article

### Census records
- **Federal census:** The first federal census was 1790 — entirely outside the 1700–1789 window. The 1790 census covers the very end of this period and can help anchor families.
- **Colonial-era enumerations:** No enumeration-style census returns were found in FamilySearch's tool results for this period. Per the FamilySearch Wiki "New York City Genealogy" article, **tax assessment lists** survive for some years and wards, and **militia rolls** list men of military age — both serve as census substitutes. See Tax Records and Military Records sections.

### Probate and court records
- **FamilySearch collection "New York, Probate Records, 1629–1971"** (collection ID 1542780) covers the full 1700s. Contains approximately 987,000 records and ~494,000 images. This is indexed and is the primary online source for wills and estate files. Access: [collection 1542780](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1542780) — indexed + images, free.
- **Prerogative Court of New York:** During the colonial period, wills for all of New York Province were proved in this court. Per the FamilySearch Wiki "New York City Genealogy" article, these are **indexed in the published *Abstracts of Wills* series issued by the New-York Historical Society** — an essential finding aid.
- **Mayor's Court:** Per the FamilySearch Wiki article, Mayor's Court records document civil litigation and may name family members, business relationships, and debts. These records are held at the New York City Municipal Archives.
- **Types available:** Wills, inventories, letters of administration, guardianship files, civil suits

### Land records
- **System:** Metes-and-bounds (colonial grant system); manorial land grants from the Dutch and British periods
- **FamilySearch collection "New York, Land Records, 1630–1975"** (collection ID 2078654) covers the full 1700s. Contains approximately 1.6 million records and ~784,000 images. Access: [collection 2078654](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2078654) — indexed + images, free.
- **Land patents and grants:** Per the FamilySearch Wiki "New York City Genealogy" article, land conveyances and patents from the colonial era are held at the New York State Archives as part of the **New York Colonial Manuscripts**. These are a critical source for property ownership and family relationships.
- **Types:** Deeds, patents, grants, mortgages, conveyances

### Military records
- **Relevant conflicts:** French and Indian War (1754–1763); American Revolution (1775–1783)
- **Colonial militia rolls:** Per the FamilySearch Wiki "New York City Genealogy" article, militia rolls survive for some periods and list men of military age by ward — useful as a substitute census.
- **Revolutionary War:** Service records, pension files, and bounty land warrants are held at **NARA** (National Archives and Records Administration) and are partially accessible via **Fold3** (subscription), which was returned as an external link for this place. Pension files can document birth dates, marriages, and family composition in remarkable detail.
- **New York State Archives:** Holds additional state-level military records for the provincial and revolutionary periods

### Cemetery records
- **Major colonial cemeteries:** Trinity Church churchyard (Anglican/Episcopal, Manhattan); Dutch Reformed church burial grounds; Jewish cemeteries (Chatham Square, 1656 — one of the oldest Jewish cemeteries in the U.S.); various ward/potter's field burials
- **Find A Grave** was returned as an external curated link for this place — useful for searching interments, though coverage of 1700s burials is variable
- **Physical sexton records:** Where they survive, held at individual churches or the NYC Municipal Archives
- **⚠️ Note:** Many colonial-era gravestones in Manhattan have been damaged or relocated; physical evidence should be cross-checked against church burial registers

### Newspaper records
- **New York Gazette:** Per the Wikipedia article, this paper was active during the colonial period and is a documented source for the 1700s. Colonial-era papers published marriage notices, death notices, legal notices (estate administration, land sales), and runaway servant/slave advertisements — all genealogically useful.
- **Newspapers.com** was returned as an external curated link for this place (subscription required)
- **Physical/microfilm:** Early colonial newspapers are held in research libraries; check the American Antiquarian Society and NYPL collections

### Immigration and naturalization records
- **Port of New York** was one of the most active colonial-era ports of entry. Passenger records from this period are fragmentary compared to later eras but do exist.
- **Naturalization:** Pre-1906 naturalizations could be filed in any court of record; for colonial NYC, check the Mayor's Court and the provincial courts. The British colonial Denization and Naturalization records are held at the New York State Archives.
- **NARA** was returned as an external curated link and holds federal-era passenger and naturalization records
- **Note:** Dutch-era immigrants and their descendants may have records in the Netherlands; English immigrants may have records in English parish registers

### Tax records
- **FamilySearch collection "New York, Tax Assessment Rolls of Real and Personal Estates, 1799–1804"** (collection ID 1678432, ~123,000 records) touches only **1799**, the very last year of the target period. It is not useful for earlier decades.
- **Colonial tax lists:** Per the FamilySearch Wiki "New York City Genealogy" article, tax assessment lists survive for **some years and wards** and are held at the New York State Archives and NYHS. These are especially valuable as census substitutes for the pre-1790 period.
- **Digitization level:** The 1799–1804 FamilySearch collection is indexed; earlier colonial tax lists are largely physical-only or microfilm.

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Collections covering this place & period | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Church Records 1640–1940 (coll. 1469062); Land Records 1630–1975 (coll. 2078654); Probate Records 1629–1971 (coll. 1542780); Dutch Reformed Church Registers 1700–1776 (vol. 007733180, browse-only, Dutch) | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census (first available: 1790); may hold additional NY church and probate collections | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Revolutionary War and French & Indian War military records | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical colonial-era newspapers | Subscription |
| **Find A Grave** | Cemetery transcriptions | Free |
| **NARA (archives.gov)** | Federal-era passenger lists, military pension files, naturalization | Free / in-person |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **New York State Archives** (Albany) | New York Colonial Manuscripts; land patents; provincial court records; militia rolls; colonial tax lists | In-person; many finding aids online at archives.nysed.gov |
| **New York City Municipal Archives** | Mayor's Court records; early city administrative records; later vital records | In-person; some digitized at the NYC Municipal Archives website |
| **New-York Historical Society** (Manhattan) | Extensive colonial-era manuscript collections; published *Abstracts of Wills* series | In-person research library; some digital content |
| **New York Genealogical & Biographical Society** | Colonial-era family files; *New York Genealogical and Biographical Record* (published journal with colonial transcriptions) | Membership / in-person; some online access |
| **Trinity Church Archives** | Anglican/Episcopal baptism, marriage, and burial registers for Trinity Church | Contact Trinity Church Wall Street directly |
| **Congregation Shearith Israel** | Jewish community records from 1654 onward | Contact congregation |

---

## Records NOT online

The following record types are documented as existing but are **not available through any online database** identified by the survey tools:

- **Most colonial-era tax assessment lists** (pre-1799) — held at NYSA and NYHS
- **Mayor's Court records** — NYC Municipal Archives (in-person)
- **Anglican/Trinity Church pre-publication registers** — Trinity Church Archives
- **Presbyterian, Lutheran, and French Huguenot registers** — scattered in denominational archives
- **Jewish community records (Shearith Israel)** — contact the congregation
- **New York Colonial Manuscripts (land patents, provincial administration)** — NYSA, some on microfilm

---

## Known record losses

- The **British occupation of New York City (1776–1783)** disrupted civilian life and record-keeping. Some records from this period are incomplete.
- The **Dutch Reformed volume on FamilySearch (007733180)** covers only 1700–1776 — coverage after 1776 through the end of the century is not represented in that digitized volume.
- No tool result identified a major courthouse fire or flood affecting NYC colonial records, but researchers should verify holdings directly with the NYC Municipal Archives, as some early records did not survive administrative transitions.

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with church records, not civil registration** — there are no civil vital records for NYC in the 1700s. The FamilySearch Church Records collection (1469062) is indexed and the natural first stop; the Dutch Reformed volume (007733180) must be browsed image-by-image and read in Dutch.

2. **The Dutch Reformed registers are in Dutch** — If your family was part of the Dutch Reformed community (very common among older New York families), you will need to read 18th-century Dutch handwriting and language, or work with a translator/specialist.

3. **Work the probate trail hard** — The *Abstracts of Wills* published by the New-York Historical Society indexes colonial wills proved in the Prerogative Court of New York. This is one of the most reliable name-indexed finding aids for the period and should be consulted before searching unindexed manuscripts.

4. **Use militia rolls and tax lists as census substitutes** — No decennial census exists before 1790. Colonial-era tax assessments (by ward) and militia rolls name adult men and can help establish residence, approximate age, and property.

5. **Follow colonial land records** — New York's colonial land grant system generated deeds and patents that often name family members, neighbors, and sellers going back generations. The Land Records collection (2078654) is indexed.

6. **The FamilySearch Wiki "New York City Genealogy" article** (https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/New_York_City_Genealogy) is the recommended reading before research planning — it identifies colonial repositories and finding aids in detail.

7. **The NY Genealogical and Biographical Record** (published by the NYG&B Society) is a key scholarly journal that has published transcriptions and abstracts of colonial NYC records for over 150 years — check it through the society's library or a subscribing research library.

8. **For Revolutionary War families**, the pension files at NARA (also searchable on Fold3) can contain detailed family histories. Many NYC men served, and widows' pension applications often document marriage dates and family structure spanning back into mid-century.

9. **Ethnic community strategy:** If your family was Dutch, French Huguenot, or Sephardic Jewish, research the specific community's institutional records as a parallel track — these groups maintained distinct congregational and organizational records with sometimes better survival rates than general civic records.

---

# Locality Guide: Virginia (American Revolutionary War, 1775–1783)

## Jurisdiction overview

- **Status during period:** Royal Colony of Virginia until 1776, then Commonwealth of Virginia (declared independence June 29, 1776 with its own constitution); formally recognized as a state with U.S. Constitution ratification in 1788
- **Administrative structure:** County courts were the fundamental unit of civil government; roughly 50–60 counties existed during this period, with new counties being created throughout the war years
- **Territory:** Virginia's 1775–1783 boundaries were vastly larger than modern Virginia — they encompassed present-day West Virginia (separated 1863), Kentucky (separated 1792), and Virginia's claims to the Northwest Territory (Ohio, Indiana, etc., ceded to the federal government in 1784). Families living in trans-Allegheny Virginia or early Kentucky settlements will have records in Virginia county books, not separate state records.
- **Population:** No direct figures returned by the population tool for this period. Virginia was the most populous of the colonies/states; the 1790 federal census (the earliest available, taken after the war) showed approximately 748,000 residents — though the Virginia portion of that census was largely destroyed (see **Known Record Losses** below).
- **Economy:** Plantation agriculture (tobacco, wheat, corn); significant maritime trade; iron manufacturing; frontier settlement and land speculation
- **Religious denominations:** Anglican/Church of England (the established church, disestablished by the Virginia Statute for Religious Freedom, 1786); Quakers; Presbyterians; Baptists (growing rapidly during this period); German Reformed and Lutheran communities in the Shenandoah Valley
- **Key historical events:** Dunmore's Proclamation (1775, offering freedom to enslaved people who fled to British lines); Virginia's declaration of independence and new state constitution (1776); major combat on Virginia soil (1781), culminating in the Siege of Yorktown (October 1781); widespread Loyalist activity and property confiscations; enlistment of free Black Virginians and, in some cases, enslaved men promised freedom for service

---

## Boundary changes affecting records

- **Pre-1776:** Virginia was organized as a Crown colony; parish vestry books (Anglican) and county court records were the primary civil record-keeping infrastructure
- **1776–1783:** New counties were carved from existing ones throughout the war; always check when your target county was formed and which parent county held records before that date. (E.g., families later found in Kentucky counties were in Fincastle County, VA before 1776, then in Kentucky County, VA from 1776–1780, before Kentucky became a state in 1792)
- **West Virginia records:** Families in the trans-Allegheny region (present-day West Virginia) had their Revolutionary-era records created in Virginia county books; those records are held today at West Virginia archives as well as the Library of Virginia

---

## Available record types

### 🎖️ Military records — the primary target

This is the richest category for your research question. Multiple distinct record classes survive:

**1. FamilySearch — "Virginia, Military Records, 1775–1783"**
- **Collection ID:** 1945678 | **Date range:** 1775–1783 (exactly on point)
- **Scale:** 345,678 records reported, 172,839 images
- **Access:** [https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1945678](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1945678) — Free
- **Digitization level:** Records and images present; name-index searchability not confirmed by tool — browse capability confirmed by image count
- **Source (wiki):** FamilySearch Wiki, *Virginia Military Records*, §Revolutionary War

**2. NARA — Continental Line service records and muster rolls**
- **Record Group:** RG 93 (War Department Collection of Revolutionary War Records)
- **Content:** Compiled service records, muster rolls, pay vouchers for Virginia Continental Army soldiers
- **Access:** National Archives, Washington DC; many records digitized and searchable on **Fold3.com** (subscription; returned by external_links_search)
- **Digitization level:** Fold3 has significant digitized coverage; physical originals at NARA

**3. NARA — Pension and bounty-land warrant application files**
- **Record Group:** RG 15
- **Content:** Post-war pension applications filed by veterans and their widows/heirs; among the most genealogically rich Revolutionary War records — often include sworn biographical statements, names of family members, service details, and witnesses. Widows' pension files frequently contain marriage dates and places.
- **Access:** Fold3.com (subscription); NARA microfilm; many indexed at Ancestry.com. Physical originals at NARA.
- **Source (wiki):** FamilySearch Wiki, *Virginia Military Records*, §Revolutionary War

**4. Library of Virginia — Virginia militia records**
- **Content:** Muster rolls, pay records, and enrollment lists for Virginia state militia (distinct from the Continental Line). Militia records are often less complete than Continental records but may be the only documentation for men who served locally without formally enlisting
- **Access:** Library of Virginia, Richmond (physical and some digitized); [https://www.lva.virginia.gov](https://www.lva.virginia.gov)
- **Digitization level:** Partially digitized; significant physical holdings remain on-site
- **Source (wiki):** FamilySearch Wiki, *Virginia Military Records*, §Revolutionary War

**5. Virginia State Navy records**
- **Content:** Published records of the Virginia State Navy (the Commonwealth's own naval forces during the Revolution, separate from the Continental Navy)
- **Access:** Published volumes available at Library of Virginia and major research libraries; some accessible through HathiTrust and Google Books
- **Source (wiki):** FamilySearch Wiki, *Virginia Military Records*, §Revolutionary War

**6. Post-war bounty land records — Virginia Military District of Ohio**
- **Content:** Virginia granted bounty lands in the Virginia Military District of Ohio to its Revolutionary War veterans. Warrant and survey records connect veterans to their specific land claims and are a key cross-reference for service documentation.
- **Access:** Virginia State Library (Library of Virginia); Ohio History Connection; some records accessible via Bureau of Land Management GLO website
- **Source (wiki):** FamilySearch Wiki, *Virginia Military Records*, §Post-War Bounty Lands

---

### Tax records — critical census substitute ⚠️

- **FamilySearch — "Virginia, Tax Records, 1782–1850"**
  - **Collection ID:** 1456723 | **Date range:** begins 1782 (overlaps end of war)
  - **Scale:** 456,789 records reported, 228,394 images
  - **Access:** [https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1456723](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1456723) — Free
- **Why essential for this period:** The 1790 federal Virginia census was largely destroyed (see Known Record Losses). County personal property tax lists from 1782 onward are the primary substitute for identifying Virginia residents immediately after the war. They name heads of household annually, list taxable property (horses, cattle, enslaved people, carriages), and can track a family's county-by-county movement. A man absent from the tax list during 1775–1781 war years may be documented in militia or service records instead.
- **Tip:** Some Virginia counties also survive from pre-1782 tithable/tithe lists; these predate the formal 1782 tax system and are held at the Library of Virginia.

---

### Land records

- **FamilySearch — "Virginia, Land, Marriage, and Will Records, 1600–1900"**
  - **Collection ID:** 1734285 | **Date range:** 1600–1900
  - **Scale:** 2,567,890 records reported, 1,283,945 images
  - **Access:** [https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1734285](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1734285) — Free
- **System:** Metes and bounds (Virginia is a state-land state; land grants originated with the Crown/Commonwealth, not the federal government)
- **Wartime-specific:** Confiscated Loyalist properties were auctioned during the war; these sales appear in deed books. Post-war veteran land grants in the Virginia Military District of Ohio also appear here.
- **Where else held:** County deed books at county courthouses; Library of Virginia holds microfilm of most county deed books; Virginia Land Office records at Library of Virginia

---

### Church records

- **FamilySearch — "Virginia, Church Records, 1680–1929"**
  - **Collection ID:** 1598230 | **Date range:** 1680–1929
  - **Scale:** 1,234,567 records reported, 617,283 images
  - **Access:** [https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1598230](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1598230) — Free
- **Denominations and context:** Anglican vestry books are the primary pre-war vital records source; they recorded births, marriages, and burials by parish. With the disestablishment of the Anglican Church during the Revolution, parish registration broke down in many areas. Baptist, Presbyterian, and Quaker records fill the gap from the late 1770s onward for those communities.
- **Quaker records:** Monthly meeting records (births, marriages, deaths, disownments for joining the military) are particularly well-preserved and often name members who were disowned for taking up arms — paradoxically making Quaker records useful for tracing men who left the meeting to serve.
- **Language:** English throughout
- **⚠️ No civil registration:** Virginia did not require statewide registration of births, marriages, or deaths until **1853** — forty years after the target period. Church records, county marriage bonds/licenses, and family bibles are the only vital record sources for 1775–1783.

---

### County court records

- **FamilySearch — "Virginia, County Court Records, 1650–1920"**
  - **Collection ID:** 2134567 | **Date range:** 1650–1920
  - **Scale:** 1,789,012 records reported, 894,506 images
  - **Access:** [https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2134567](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2134567) — Free
- **Wartime-specific content:** County court order books during 1775–1783 document oaths of allegiance required of residents, appointments of militia officers, processing of wartime claims and damages, guardianship of orphans of soldiers killed in service, and Loyalist property proceedings. These are underused but rich sources for wartime activities.
- **Physical holdings:** Library of Virginia holds microfilm of most Virginia county court records; county courthouses retain original deed and will books; some counties have deposited older records with the Library of Virginia

---

### Vital records (civil registration)

- **⚠️ Does not apply to this period.** Statewide civil registration of births, marriages, and deaths did not begin in Virginia until 1853.
- **Marriage bonds and licenses:** Before civil registration, county clerks issued marriage bonds (a financial guarantee) and licenses. These survive for many counties from the colonial period onward and are included in the land/marriage/will collection above.
- **Substitute sources:** Anglican/Episcopal vestry books (pre-disestablishment), Baptist and Presbyterian church registers, family Bibles, county court order books for guardianship and estate proceedings naming heirs

---

### Census records

- **⚠️ No federal census during the war years.** The first U.S. federal census was 1790, after the war's end.
- **1790 Virginia census — LARGELY DESTROYED** (see Known Record Losses below). Tax lists from 1782–1789 are the direct substitute.
- **1800 and later censuses** are intact and may help establish families one generation forward from your 1775–1783 subjects.

---

### Cemetery and burial records

- **FindAGrave** (returned by external_links_search): [https://www.findagrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com) — Free; extensive Virginia coverage including colonial and Revolutionary-era family cemeteries
- **Context:** Many Revolutionary War burials are in family farm cemeteries, churchyards, or battlefield sites. The DAR (Daughters of the American Revolution) has surveyed many Virginia cemeteries; their indexes are available at DAR Library in Washington DC and some state chapters.

---

### Newspapers

- **Newspapers.com** (returned by external_links_search): [https://www.newspapers.com](https://www.newspapers.com) — Subscription
- **Virginia newspapers during the war:** *Virginia Gazette* (Williamsburg, multiple competing editions during the Revolution) is the primary colonial/early state paper. It published military notices, calls to arms, lists of deserters, Loyalist proceedings, and estate notices. Available on microfilm and partially digitized.
- **Research value:** Notices of military musters, descriptions of deserters (with physical descriptions useful for identification), sheriff's sale notices for confiscated Loyalist estates, and mortality notices

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Collections covering Virginia 1775–1783 | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | "Virginia, Military Records, 1775–1783" (345,678 records); "Virginia, Land, Marriage, and Will Records, 1600–1900" (2,567,890 records); "Virginia, Church Records, 1680–1929" (1,234,567 records); "Virginia, Tax Records, 1782–1850" (456,789 records); "Virginia, County Court Records, 1650–1920" (1,789,012 records) | Free |
| **Fold3** | Continental Line service records, pension applications, Virginia militia records | Subscription |
| **Ancestry.com** | Pension files, published rosters, some Virginia county records | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** | Virginia cemetery records, including Revolutionary-era markers | Free |
| **Newspapers.com** | *Virginia Gazette* and other colonial/early state papers | Subscription |
| **NARA (archives.gov)** | RG 93 (service records), RG 15 (pension files) — many digitized via Fold3/Ancestry | Varies |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Key holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Library of Virginia**, Richmond | Virginia militia records; pre-1782 tithables; county court microfilm; land office records; Loyalist claims; Virginia Military District warrant records | In-person; some records online via [lva.virginia.gov](https://www.lva.virginia.gov) |
| **National Archives**, Washington DC | RG 93 (Continental service/muster rolls), RG 15 (pension files), RG 360 (Continental Congress papers) | In-person or order copies; many records on Fold3 |
| **Virginia County Courthouses** | Original deed books, will books, court order books, marriage bonds — still held locally for many counties | In-person or mail request |
| **DAR Library**, Washington DC | Lineage files, published rosters, cemetery surveys, compiled Revolutionary War records | In-person; some indexes online |
| **Virginia Historical Society (Virginia Museum of History & Culture)**, Richmond | Manuscripts, family papers, published compiled rosters | In-person |

---

## Records NOT online

- **Pre-1782 tithable lists** (the colonial-era tax/population enumeration predating the formal 1782 tax system): held at Library of Virginia — physical and microfilm; limited digitization
- **Virginia State Navy records**: largely in published volumes at research libraries; not fully digitized
- **Original county deed books and will books**: many counties retain originals; Library of Virginia microfilm is the standard access point; not all are in the FamilySearch digitized collections
- **Loyalist claims and confiscation proceedings**: at Library of Virginia; not comprehensively digitized
- **Virginia Auditor's accounts** (claims for wartime services, supply requisitions, property damages): at Library of Virginia

---

## Known record losses

- **1790 Virginia federal census**: Largely destroyed when the British burned Washington in 1814 (per FamilySearch Wiki, *Virginia Military Records*). County tax lists from 1782 onward are the standard substitute — begin your census-substitute research there.
- **County courthouse fires**: Virginia suffered significant county record losses throughout the 19th century (and some during the Civil War). Before investing heavily in a county, check whether its pre-1800 records survive. The Library of Virginia maintains a county-by-county survey of record losses.
- **Anglican vestry books**: With the disestablishment of the Church of England during the Revolution, some vestry books were abandoned, lost, or deliberately destroyed. Coverage is uneven county to county.

---

## Research tips

1. **Work pension files first.** Pension application files (NARA RG 15, accessible via Fold3/Ancestry) are the single richest source for Revolutionary War genealogy — veterans and widows submitted sworn statements that often include ages, birthplaces, marriage information, and witness names. If your ancestor served, this is where to start.

2. **Tax lists bridge the census gap.** Since the 1790 Virginia census was destroyed, the 1782–1789 personal property tax lists (FamilySearch collection 1456723) are essential for placing a family in a specific county immediately after the war. Run the tax lists year by year from 1782 through 1800 to track movement.

3. **Identify the correct county — and its formation date.** Virginia had dozens of counties, many newly created during the 1770s–1780s. A man who appears in one county in 1775 may be in a newly formed daughter county by 1783. Always check the Library of Virginia's *A Hornbook of Virginia History* or the FamilySearch Wiki for Virginia county formation dates.

4. **Look for the family across the boundary to Ohio.** Virginia's bounty land grants were in the Virginia Military District of Ohio (southern Ohio). Veterans or their heirs who claimed bounty land will have warrant and survey records at both the Library of Virginia and the Ohio History Connection — this trail often connects a Virginia service record to a later Ohio family.

5. **Quaker records document conscientious objectors.** Men who refused to serve were often disowned by their Monthly Meeting; this disownment is recorded in meeting minutes. Paradoxically, men from Quaker families who did serve may appear in disownment records — a form of negative evidence useful for confirming military activity.

6. **Check for Loyalist proceedings.** County court records and the Library of Virginia's sequestration and confiscation records name both patriots (who brought charges) and Loyalists (whose property was seized). Either role documents wartime activity.

7. **DAR lineage files as secondary sources.** The Daughters of the American Revolution has approved thousands of Virginia lineage files. These are secondary sources requiring verification, but they often identify the specific service record or pension file a previous researcher located — useful as a finding aid to primary sources.

8. **FamilySearch Wiki article to consult:** [Virginia Military Records](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Virginia_Military_Records#Revolutionary_War) — covers Continental Line, militia, Navy, pensions, and bounty lands in detail.

---

# Locality Guide: New Orleans, Orleans Parish, Louisiana (c. 1800–1840)

## Jurisdiction Overview

| | |
|---|---|
| **Founded** | 1718 (French colonial town) |
| **Administrative unit** | Orleans Parish (coterminous with the city; Louisiana uses *parishes*, not counties) |
| **Legal tradition** | Civil law (Napoleonic Code), not English common law — fundamentally shapes record types |
| **Political transitions during period** | Spanish colony → briefly French (1800–1803) → U.S. Orleans Territory (1803–1812) → State of Louisiana (1812–) |
| **Population** | No fixture data returned; the city was one of the largest in the U.S. by 1820 |
| **Economy** | Major port; cotton and sugar trade; enslaved labor; river commerce |
| **Ethnic composition** | French Creole, Spanish Creole, free people of color, enslaved Africans, Anglo-American immigrants, German and Irish settlers arriving through the 1820s–1830s |
| **Religious denominations** | Roman Catholic dominant (St. Louis Cathedral, founded 1727); Protestant congregations present by the 1820s |
| **Key historical events affecting records** | Great Fire of 1788 (856 buildings destroyed), Fire of 1794 (~200 buildings), recurring yellow fever epidemics, hurricanes, Battle of New Orleans (1815) |

---

## Boundary Changes and Jurisdictional Continuity

The target period straddles three governmental regimes — Spanish colonial (ending 1803), U.S. Orleans Territory (1803–1812), and Louisiana statehood (1812 onward). This is a critical research trail: records created under Spanish authority are held separately from those created under U.S. territorial authority. Treat them as one continuous trail for the same population, not as unrelated record sets. The **Louisiana State Archives (Baton Rouge)** holds territorial-era records; the **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** holds notarial acts across all three regimes from 1734 forward.

---

## The Disaster Picture — What Was Lost and What Survived

### What Was Lost
- The **Great Fire of 1788** destroyed approximately 856 buildings in the French Quarter and wiped out most French colonial-era administrative records created before that date.
- The **Fire of 1794** destroyed approximately 200 additional buildings.
- Repeated **hurricanes, floods, and yellow fever epidemics** further damaged archival holdings throughout the 19th century.
- Result: researchers will find significant gaps for the pre-1800 French colonial period, and some gaps in records created in the immediate aftermath of 1788 and 1794.

### What Survived — the Core Sources for Early 1800s Research
The following record types are explicitly confirmed in tool results as survivors and the backbone of New Orleans research for this period:

1. **St. Louis Cathedral sacramental registers** — Baptisms, marriages, and burials recorded from 1731 forward (with gaps). These survived the fires and are the single most important substitute for civil vital registration for this period. Records are in French and Spanish for the colonial era, transitioning to English after 1803.

2. **Notarial records** — Louisiana's civil law tradition required that contracts, sales, donations, successions, manumissions, and other legal acts be executed before a licensed notary, who retained a permanent protocol copy. The **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** holds over 30 million pages of notarial acts dating to 1734. Because the civil law system built in redundancy (multiple copies), these records survived the fires more robustly than administrative records.

3. **Cabildo records** — Records of the Spanish colonial governing body (the Cabildo) survived and document governance, property, and population in the Spanish colonial period (pre-1803), providing context for people appearing in the early 1800s.

4. **Louisiana State Archives (Baton Rouge)** — Holds territorial (1803–1812) and early state records.

---

## Available Record Types

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)
- **Start date:** Louisiana did not have statewide civil registration during this period. No civil birth or death registration system existed at the state level for the early 1800s.
- **What exists:** Limited local civil marriage records in Orleans Parish began before statehood; consult the notarial records and parish civil registers.
- **Where held:** Louisiana State Archives (Baton Rouge) for territorial records; Orleans Parish Civil District Court for local civil records.
- **Pre-registration alternatives (critical):** St. Louis Cathedral sacramental registers (baptisms, marriages, burials from 1731) are the primary substitute — see Church Records below.

### Church Records ⭐ PRIMARY SOURCE FOR THIS PERIOD
- **Denominations:** Roman Catholic dominant; St. Louis Cathedral (founded 1727) recorded the vast majority of the population.
- **Record types:** Baptisms, marriages, burials — these are the functional vital records for 1800–1840 New Orleans.
- **Language:** French and Spanish through the early period; increasingly English after 1803. **Researchers must be prepared to read French- and Spanish-language records.**
- **FamilySearch collection:** **Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925** (collection [1612905](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1612905)) — 987,654 records with 493,827 images. **Covers the full 1800–1840 window.** Digitization level: indexed + images (name-searchable).
- **Physical originals:** Archdiocesan Archives, New Orleans; some volumes also held at the Louisiana State Archives.
- **Coverage note:** The wiki confirms "gaps" in the registers due to fires and other losses — when a sacramental register entry is missing, move immediately to the notarial records.

### Notarial Records ⭐ UNIQUE TO LOUISIANA — ESSENTIAL SUBSTITUTE
- **What they are:** Legal acts executed before a notary: property sales, successions (probate), donations, marriage contracts, manumissions of enslaved persons, tutorships, interdictions, and commercial agreements. In Louisiana's civil law system, these are far more comprehensive than in common-law states.
- **Date range:** 1734 to present at the New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center (30M+ pages).
- **FamilySearch digitized volume:** imageGroup **004990115** — 167 images of notarial acts, covering Orleans, Louisiana, **1803–1820**. ⚠️ **Browse-only: 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable. Must be browsed image by image. Records are in French and English — reading knowledge of French required.**
- **Physical repository:** New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center (in-person or written inquiry).
- **Research value as disaster substitute:** Because notarial protocols document property transactions and family relationships, they can reconstruct facts that would otherwise appear in destroyed vital or civil records.

### Probate and Succession Records
- **Louisiana term:** "Successions" (not "probate") — Louisiana's civil law terminology.
- **Also:** Tutorships (guardianship of minors) and interdictions (guardianship of incapacitated adults) — record types unique to Louisiana's civil law system.
- **FamilySearch collection:** **Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984** (collection [2078341](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2078341)) — 456,789 records, 228,394 images. Covers the full 1800–1840 window. Digitization level: indexed + images.
- **Research value:** Successions name heirs, relationships, and property — essential when vital records are absent.
- **Physical repository:** Orleans Parish Civil District Court (original volumes); Louisiana State Archives (some copies).

### Census Records
- **Federal:** The 1810, 1820, 1830, and 1840 federal censuses all enumerate New Orleans. These survive and are available on FamilySearch and Ancestry. (Note: the 1800 census for Orleans Territory is limited; Louisiana was a U.S. territory from 1803.)
- **FamilySearch:** U.S. federal censuses are indexed and searchable. Access via [Ancestry.com](https://www.ancestry.com/search/collections/census/) (subscription) or FamilySearch free collections.
- **Territorial census:** Some territorial enumerations for Orleans Territory (pre-1812) exist; consult the Louisiana State Archives.

### Death Records
- **FamilySearch collection:** **Louisiana, Statewide Death Index, 1819–1964** (collection [1543892](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1543892)) — 2,345,678 index entries; **0 images** (index only, no original images linked). Partially overlaps the target period (from 1819). Digitization level: index only — use as a pointer to the underlying physical record.
- **Note:** Yellow fever epidemics caused mass-casualty events; mortality was recorded inconsistently. Cross-reference with church burial registers.

### Marriage Records
- **FamilySearch collection:** **Louisiana, Parish Marriages, 1837–1957** (collection [1798054](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1798054)) — 567,890 records, 283,945 images. Begins 1837, so it covers only the **tail end** of the 1800–1840 window. For marriages 1800–1836, rely on St. Louis Cathedral marriage registers (collection 1612905) and notarial marriage contracts.

### Land Records
- **System:** Louisiana uses a French/Spanish land grant system (arpents, toises), not the U.S. rectangular survey system.
- **Record types:** Land grants, concessions, sales via notarial act, and tax records.
- **Federal land patents:** Bureau of Land Management General Land Office (GLO) website for federal-era grants post-1803.
- **Notarial acts:** Property sales are in the notarial record corpus at the New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center.
- **Physical:** Orleans Parish Conveyance Office (deed records); Louisiana State Land Office.

### Military Records
- **Relevant conflict:** Battle of New Orleans (January 1815) — War of 1812. Many New Orleans residents served.
- **Record types:** Service records and pension files at NARA; War of 1812 pension files are on [Fold3](https://www.fold3.com/) (subscription) and FamilySearch.
- **Louisiana militia:** Territorial militia records at Louisiana State Archives.

### Immigration and Port Records
- **Context:** New Orleans was the busiest port of entry in the South during this period. Large numbers of immigrants from France, Germany, Ireland, and the Caribbean arrived.
- **Passenger lists:** NARA holds arrival manifests; available via [Ancestry.com](https://www.ancestry.com/) and FamilySearch. Note: early 1800s passenger lists are less detailed than later lists.
- **Naturalization:** Pre-1906 naturalizations could be filed in any court of record — check Orleans Parish District Court records at Louisiana State Archives.

### Cemetery Records
- **Context:** New Orleans's distinctive above-ground burial culture; major historic cemeteries include St. Louis Cemetery No. 1 (1789), St. Louis Cemetery No. 2 (1823), and Lafayette Cemetery No. 1 (1833).
- **Online:** [FindAGrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com/) has coverage for New Orleans cemeteries.
- **Physical records:** Interment registers at archdiocesan archives and individual cemetery offices; some digitized at the New Orleans Archdiocesan Archives.
- **Research value:** Burial dates and informant names can substitute for missing death records.

### Newspaper Records
- **Context:** New Orleans had active newspapers from the early 1800s (e.g., *Le Moniteur de la Louisiane*, founded 1794; *Louisiana Gazette*, 1804). Obituaries, marriage notices, and legal notices appeared regularly.
- **Online access:** [Newspapers.com](https://www.newspapers.com/) (subscription); Louisiana Digital Library; Library of Congress *Chronicling America* (free, partial coverage).
- **Research value:** Legal notices (successions, sheriff's sales) and social notices (births, marriages, deaths) appear even when no formal registration existed.

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | Collections for 1800–1840 New Orleans | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Louisiana, Church Records 1718–1925 (987,654 records); Louisiana, Probate Records 1756–1984 (456,789 records); Louisiana, Statewide Death Index 1819–1964 (2,345,678 index entries); Louisiana, Parish Marriages 1837–1957 (283,945 images); Notarial Acts volume 004990115 (167 browse-only images, 1803–1820) | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census (1810, 1820, 1830, 1840); passenger lists; War of 1812 records | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Military service records; War of 1812 pension files | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical New Orleans newspapers | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** | New Orleans cemetery records | Free |
| **NARA (archives.gov)** | Federal territorial records; naturalization; military | Free/in-person |
| **Louisiana Digital Library** | State digitization projects; some newspaper holdings | Free |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** | 30M+ pages of notarial acts from 1734 — the most comprehensive survival of pre-civil-registration New Orleans documentation | In-person; written inquiry |
| **Louisiana State Archives, Baton Rouge** | Territorial records (1803–1812); early state records; some succession and court records | In-person; some online via state portal |
| **Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives** | Original St. Louis Cathedral registers; archdiocesan administrative records | In-person; written inquiry |
| **Orleans Parish Civil District Court** | Local civil records; succession files; naturalization records | In-person |
| **Louisiana State Museum (The Cabildo)** | Spanish colonial government records; artifacts and documents | In-person; research inquiries |
| **New Orleans Public Library, Louisiana Division** | City directories; local histories; newspaper morgue | In-person |
| **NARA Southeast Region (Atlanta)** | Federal records for Louisiana territory and state | In-person; NARA online catalog |

---

## Records NOT Online

- **New Orleans Notarial Archives (bulk):** Only 167 images (1803–1820) are on FamilySearch as a browse-only volume. The full corpus of 30M+ pages is held physically at the Notarial Archives Research Center — no comprehensive online access exists.
- **Cabildo administrative records:** Physical at the Louisiana State Museum; not comprehensively digitized.
- **Orleans Parish civil court records** (original volumes): Must be accessed in person or by written inquiry.
- **Archdiocesan Archives originals:** The FamilySearch collection (1612905) covers a broad range, but gaps exist; original volumes at the Archdiocesan Archives may fill them.
- **Territorial militia records (pre-1812):** Louisiana State Archives; not known to be digitized.

---

## Known Record Losses and Substitute Sources

| Disaster | What Was Lost | Best Substitutes |
|---|---|---|
| **Fire of 1788** (856 buildings) | Most French colonial administrative records pre-1788 | St. Louis Cathedral registers (1731–, survived); Spanish Cabildo records (post-1769); Notarial archives (1734–, largely survived) |
| **Fire of 1794** (~200 buildings) | Additional colonial-era civil records | Same as above; some records reconstructed by Spanish administration |
| **Yellow fever epidemics** | Underregistration of deaths; loss of institutional memory | Church burial registers; newspaper death notices; succession records filed after death |
| **Hurricanes and floods (ongoing)** | Periodic damage to physical record repositories | Microfilm copies held at FamilySearch Family History Library; Louisiana State Archives duplicate holdings |

**Key principle for New Orleans research:** Because the fires predated 1800, the early 1800s records themselves largely survive — the challenge is that *pre-1800 background records* helping to identify individuals may be missing. The notarial records are the most important compensating source because the civil law system required notarial documentation for virtually every significant legal act in a person's life.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Lead with the notarial records.** In no other U.S. city do notarial archives substitute so thoroughly for missing vital and civil records. A property sale, succession, or marriage contract can establish parentage, relationships, and dates of birth with precision.

2. **Expect multilingual records.** French dominates through the early 1800s; Spanish appears in colonial-era notarial acts (pre-1803); English becomes common after the Louisiana Purchase but is not universal until the 1820s–1830s. Plan for translation needs. The single digitized notarial volume on FamilySearch (004990115) is in French and English.

3. **The FamilySearch notarial volume is browse-only.** imageGroup 004990115 (167 images, 1803–1820) has 0% name-indexing and is not full-text searchable — you must browse it image by image. Use it as a sample; the full corpus is at the Notarial Archives Research Center.

4. **Work all three government regimes.** For any individual present in New Orleans around 1803, search Spanish colonial records (pre-1803), territorial records (1803–1812), and Louisiana state records (1812+) as one continuous trail — the population is the same even though the government changed.

5. **The St. Louis Cathedral collection on FamilySearch (1612905) is your first stop for vital events.** It is name-indexed with images and covers 1718–1925, giving full coverage of the 1800–1840 target window.

6. **For free people of color and enslaved persons**, notarial records of manumission and the St. Louis Cathedral registers (which recorded baptisms for enslaved and free Black parishioners) are critical — and survive for this period.

7. **The Louisiana, Statewide Death Index 1819–1964 (collection 1543892)** provides 2.3M index entries but no linked images — use it to establish a date and then retrieve the underlying record from physical repositories.

8. **Consult the FamilySearch Wiki page "New Orleans Genealogy"** ([direct link](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/New_Orleans_Genealogy#Records_Surviving_Disasters)) for the dedicated section on records surviving disasters — the wiki_search confirmed this section exists with detailed guidance.

9. **For Spanish colonial background records** (ancestors present pre-1803), the Archivo General de Indias (Seville, Spain) holds some Louisiana colonial documentation; consult the Louisiana State Archives and the Historic New Orleans Collection for finding aids.

10. **New Orleans city directories** begin in the early 1800s and can substitute for missing census years — held at the New Orleans Public Library Louisiana Division.

---

*Coverage note: The FamilySearch wiki_place_page tool returned only generic content for Louisiana rather than state-specific guidance, and wiki_read resolved to a generic genealogy records article rather than the dedicated New Orleans Genealogy page. The research tips section above draws on the wiki_search results, which did return specific New Orleans content. For the most current digitization status of Louisiana collections, consult the [FamilySearch Louisiana research page](https://www.familysearch.org/en/research/places/?text=Louisiana&focusedId=46) directly.*

---

# Locality Guide: Port of Boston Immigration, 1840s
**Scope:** Boston, Suffolk County, Massachusetts | Target period: 1840–1855

---

## Historical & Jurisdictional Context

Boston was one of the five principal U.S. ports of entry during the 1840s and the dominant receiving port for immigrants from the British Isles. The Great Famine (1845–1852) triggered a massive surge — Wikipedia notes that by 1850, over **one quarter of Boston's residents were Irish-born**, with newcomers concentrating in the North End and Fort Hill neighborhoods. German and other northern European immigrants also arrived in smaller but significant numbers.

**Key administrative facts affecting records:**
- Massachusetts began **statewide civil vital registration in 1842** — births and marriages from that year forward exist at the state level; pre-1842 events are town-level church and town clerk records only.
- Passenger arrivals were recorded under **federal Customs law** (NARA Record Group 36), not state law — the originals are federal records, not Massachusetts state records.
- Pre-1906 naturalizations could occur in **any court** — federal, state, or local — so naturalization records for this period are scattered across multiple courthouses.

---

## Records Survey

### 1. 🚢 Passenger Lists — *PRIMARY SOURCE*
**Digitization level: Full-text searchable; only ~3% name-indexed (near browse-only)**

| What | Detail |
|---|---|
| FamilySearch Collection | *Massachusetts, Passenger Lists, 1820–1891* — Collection ID 1784312, 1,234,567 records |
| Digitized Volume | Image group **003345210** — Port of Boston Passenger Lists, 1840–1850 — **389 images**, 3% name-indexed, **full-text searchable** |
| Record content | 1840s Customs lists record name, age, sex, occupation, and country of origin only — not the detailed place-of-origin, relatives named, and physical descriptions that post-1895 manifests provide |
| Originals | **National Archives at Boston (Waltham, MA)**, NARA Record Group 36 |
| Also indexed at | Ancestry.com |

⚠️ **Researcher note:** Because image group 003345210 is only 3% name-indexed, you cannot reliably find an ancestor via the normal FamilySearch name search. Use FamilySearch's **full-text search** for this volume, or browse the 389 images sequentially by estimated arrival date. The Ancestry.com index of this same collection may give better name-search coverage — check both.

---

### 2. 📰 Boston Pilot Missing Persons Database — *CRITICAL for Irish arrivals*
**Access: Free online**

The Pilot, Boston's Irish Catholic newspaper, published missing-persons notices placed by families seeking relatives who had emigrated and lost contact. These advertisements sometimes name the Irish townland or parish of origin — information rarely found in the passenger lists themselves.

- **Database:** *Boston Pilot Missing Persons Database*, 1831–1921
- **URL:** [infowanted.bc.edu](http://infowanted.bc.edu) (Boston College)
- Covers **1831–1921**; the 1840s–1850s Irish Famine influx is heavily represented

---

### 3. 📋 Vital Records
**Digitization level: Indexed + images**

| What | Detail |
|---|---|
| FamilySearch Collection | *Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620–1890* — Collection ID 2061550, **3,456,789 records** |
| Coverage | Statewide civil registration begins **1842**; prior records are at the town level |
| Physical repositories | Massachusetts State Archives (Boston); New England Historic Genealogical Society (NEHGS) |

After arrival, immigrants' marriages, children's births, and deaths were recorded in Boston (Suffolk County) town records and, after 1842, in state registration. These are key for connecting a passenger to a Boston household.

---

### 4. ⛪ Church Records — *Essential for pre-1842 and Catholic immigrants*
**Digitization level: Indexed + images (partial)**

| What | Detail |
|---|---|
| FamilySearch Collection | *Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630–1920* — Collection ID 1921657, **987,654 records** |
| Key Boston Catholic parishes | Cathedral of the Holy Cross; St. Patrick's — baptism, marriage, and burial registers from the 1840s onward |
| Physical repository | **Archdiocese of Boston Archives** — holds original parish registers |
| Wiki source | FamilySearch Wiki, *Massachusetts Church Records*, §Boston Area Churches |

For Irish Catholic immigrants especially, Catholic parish registers are often the richest post-arrival record, capturing baptisms of children born in Boston and marriage entries that may include Irish origin details.

---

### 5. 🏛️ Naturalization Records
**Digitization level: Indexed + images (partial)**

| What | Detail |
|---|---|
| FamilySearch Collection | *Massachusetts, Naturalization Records, 1790–1906* — Collection ID 1459213, **345,678 records** |
| What they contain | Declaration of intent ("first papers") and petition for citizenship; may name country/city of origin, occupation, arrival date, and personal description |
| Courts involved | Suffolk County Superior Court, U.S. Circuit/District Courts (Boston) — also local police and municipal courts |
| Physical repositories | Massachusetts State Archives; NARA Waltham (federal courts) |

Pre-1906 naturalizations are scattered — if your ancestor is not in this FamilySearch collection, also check individual court record series at the Massachusetts State Archives and NARA Waltham.

---

### 6. 🗳️ Census Records
**Digitization level: Indexed + images (Ancestry, FamilySearch)**

| Census | Relevance | Key limitation |
|---|---|---|
| 1840 Federal Census | Confirms household presence | Names **head of household only** — individual immigrants not identifiable |
| **1850 Federal Census** | First to **name every person** + list birthplace | Essential for locating an immigrant family in Boston |
| 1855 Massachusetts State Census | State-level enumeration | May provide additional detail between federal censuses |

The 1850 census is the single most powerful post-arrival locating tool for 1840s immigrants. Look for Suffolk County, Massachusetts schedules. Available on **Ancestry.com** (FamilySearch also has images).

---

### 7. ⚖️ Probate Records
**Digitization level: Indexed + images**

| What | Detail |
|---|---|
| FamilySearch Collection | *Massachusetts, Probate Records, 1635–1991* — Collection ID 1678901, **567,890 records** |
| Jurisdiction | Suffolk County Probate Court (Boston) |
| Value | Wills and administrations name heirs, sometimes noting birthplace or family members still in Ireland/Germany/etc. |

---

### 8. 📰 Newspapers
**Access: Newspapers.com (subscription)**

Boston newspapers of the 1840s — including the *Boston Herald* and *Boston Post* — carried **passenger arrival notices** listing ship names and sometimes passenger names. Wikipedia notes these are an important supplement to the formal customs lists.

- **Newspapers.com** holds historical Boston newspaper runs for this period
- Also useful for death and marriage notices after settlement

---

### 9. 🎖️ Military Records
**Access: Fold3 (subscription); NARA Waltham**

Some 1840s immigrants enlisted in the U.S. Army, particularly for the **Mexican-American War (1846–1848)**. Service records and pension files at NARA (Fold3) can confirm an immigrant was in the United States by a specific year and may name place of birth.

---

## Repository Quick Reference

| Repository | What's There | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Passenger lists, vitals, church, naturalization, probate — 5 indexed collections | Free |
| **National Archives at Boston (Waltham, MA)** | Original NARA RG 36 passenger lists; federal court naturalizations | In-person / Scan on demand |
| **Massachusetts State Archives** | State vital records (post-1842); state court naturalizations | In-person / Some online via NEHGS |
| **NEHGS** (americanancestors.org) | Major MA genealogical holdings; vital records; Pilot database partner | Subscription |
| **Archdiocese of Boston Archives** | Catholic parish registers (1840s onward) | By appointment |
| **Boston College** (infowanted.bc.edu) | Boston Pilot Missing Persons Database | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | Passenger lists (indexed); U.S. Federal Census | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical Boston newspapers | Subscription |
| **Find A Grave** (findagrave.com) | Cemetery & burial records | Free |
| **Fold3** (fold3.com) | Military service & pension records | Subscription |

---

## Recommended Research Strategy

1. **Start with the 1850 Federal Census** (Ancestry or FamilySearch) — it names every person and gives birthplace, putting your ancestor in a Boston household.
2. **Search the FamilySearch passenger list collection** (ID 1784312) by name, and use **full-text search** on image group 003345210 for the 1840–1850 volume, since it is only 3% name-indexed.
3. **Cross-check Ancestry.com's** passenger list index — it may offer better name coverage for the same underlying lists.
4. **Check the Boston Pilot Missing Persons Database** at infowanted.bc.edu if your ancestor is Irish — this is the best source for Irish townland/parish of origin.
5. **Search Catholic parish registers** for post-arrival vital events; contact the Archdiocese of Boston Archives for undigitized volumes.
6. **Pull naturalization records** — declarations of intent often name the exact country and city of origin and give arrival details.
7. **Visit or request scans from NARA Waltham** if FamilySearch/Ancestry indexes don't surface your ancestor — the original passenger lists must be browsed when indexes fail.

---

## Coverage Gaps to Note

- **1840s passenger list fields are minimal** — name, age, sex, occupation, and country of origin; no ship's destination address, no named relatives, no specific place of origin within a country.
- **The digitized passenger list volume (003345210) is only 3% name-indexed** — full-text and browse methods are required.
- **Pre-1842 vital events** are town-level only; church registers are the substitute.
- **No statewide immigration register** was kept — arrivals are in federal customs records only.
- Population data for Boston in this period was not returned by the population tool — consult NEHGS or Wikipedia for demographic context.
